question: Under EPA regulations, what is the maximum refrigerant charge that defines a 'small appliance' (Type I)?
options: 10 pounds
5 pounds
15 pounds
3 pounds
answer_text: 5 pounds
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.152, a 'small appliance' is defined as any appliance that is fully manufactured, charged, and hermetically sealed in a factory with 5 pounds or less of refrigerant. This includes household refrigerators, freezers, room air conditioners, and packaged terminal air conditioners. Systems with more than 5 pounds fall under Type II (high-pressure) or Type III (low-pressure) categories.

question: Which of the following appliances is classified as a Type I (small appliance) under EPA regulations?
options: A 20-ton commercial rooftop AC unit
A household refrigerator with a factory-sealed hermetic system containing 3 oz of R-134a
A split-system heat pump containing 6 pounds of R-410A
A large commercial freezer with a 10-pound refrigerant charge
answer_text: A household refrigerator with a factory-sealed hermetic system containing 3 oz of R-134a
explanation: A household refrigerator is a classic Type I small appliance: it is factory-sealed, hermetically sealed, and contains well under 5 pounds of refrigerant (typically a few ounces). The 20-ton rooftop unit and 6-lb heat pump are Type II (high-pressure over 5 lbs), and the 10-lb commercial freezer also exceeds the 5-lb Type I threshold per 40 CFR §82.152.

question: A technician is about to service a small window air conditioner. What feature, by definition, qualifies it as a Type I small appliance?
options: It uses CFC-12, which is a Class I ozone-depleting substance
It is a portable unit weighing less than 50 pounds
It was factory-manufactured, hermetically sealed, and charged with 5 pounds or less of refrigerant
It uses a rotary or scroll compressor instead of a reciprocating compressor
answer_text: It was factory-manufactured, hermetically sealed, and charged with 5 pounds or less of refrigerant
explanation: The three defining characteristics of a Type I small appliance per 40 CFR §82.152 are: (1) fully manufactured in a factory, (2) hermetically sealed, and (3) charged with 5 pounds or less of refrigerant. The refrigerant type, appliance weight, and compressor design are not the defining criteria for the Type I classification.

question: Which list contains ONLY Type I small appliances?
options: Household refrigerator, window AC unit, portable dehumidifier
Central air conditioner, commercial walk-in cooler, mini-split system
Centrifugal chiller, absorption chiller, screw chiller
Commercial reach-in cooler with 8 lbs of refrigerant, roof-top unit, split system
answer_text: Household refrigerator, window AC unit, portable dehumidifier
explanation: Household refrigerators, window AC units, and portable dehumidifiers are all factory-sealed hermetic systems with 5 pounds or less of refrigerant — classic Type I small appliances per 40 CFR §82.152. Central AC, mini-splits, and commercial equipment typically contain more than 5 lbs. Centrifugal, absorption, and screw chillers are large low-pressure or high-pressure systems (Type III or II).

question: A technician encounters an older window AC unit that appears to have had refrigerant added after leaving the factory. The original factory charge was 2 lbs of R-22, but the unit now contains 6 lbs. How should the technician classify this unit?
options: Still Type I, because the original factory charge was under 5 lbs
Type II, because the current charge exceeds 5 lbs regardless of the original factory charge
Type III, because R-22 is a low-pressure refrigerant classified under Type III
The classification depends on whether the compressor is functional
answer_text: Type II, because the current charge exceeds 5 lbs regardless of the original factory charge
explanation: While the Type I small appliance definition refers to factory charge, once a system contains more than 5 lbs of refrigerant (regardless of how it got there), the technician must apply recovery requirements appropriate to the actual system charge. EPA regulations and inspection standards focus on the actual refrigerant present, not the nameplate rating. A 6-lb system must meet Type II recovery requirements per 40 CFR §82.156(a).

question: Which refrigerant is most commonly found in modern household refrigerators classified as Type I small appliances?
explanation: Modern household refrigerators (manufactured after 1994) use R-134a as the replacement for R-12 (CFC-12), which was phased out in 1996. R-134a is a non-ozone-depleting HFC refrigerant widely used in small hermetically sealed appliances. R-22 is used in HVAC systems (Type II), and R-410A is used in modern high-pressure HVAC equipment, not household refrigerators.

question: A post-November 15, 1993 small appliance with a functional compressor contains 8 oz of refrigerant. What is the minimum amount of refrigerant that must be recovered before opening the system?
options: All 8 oz (100%)
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(i), technicians servicing small appliances manufactured after November 15, 1993 with a functional compressor must recover at least 90% of the refrigerant. For an 8-oz system: 8 oz × 0.90 = 7.2 oz minimum. The 90% standard applies specifically to post-1993 appliances with operable compressors.

question: A technician recovers 4.5 lbs of refrigerant from a 1998 window AC unit (post-1993) with a functional compressor. The unit has a 5-lb charge. Did the technician meet the recovery requirement?
options: Yes — 4.5 lbs is exactly 90% of 5 lbs
No — the technician needed to recover at least 4.75 lbs (95%)
Yes — recovering over 80% is always sufficient for small appliances
No — 4.5 lbs is only 90%, and post-1993 systems require 95% recovery
answer_text: Yes — 4.5 lbs is exactly 90% of 5 lbs
explanation: 4.5 lbs ÷ 5 lbs = 90%. Per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(i), the minimum recovery requirement for a post-November 15, 1993 small appliance with a functional compressor is exactly 90%. Recovering exactly 90% meets the standard. There is no 95% requirement for Type I appliances; the 90% threshold is the applicable standard.

question: A technician recovers only 85% of the refrigerant from a 2005 room air conditioner (post-1993) with a functional compressor before opening the system. Has the technician violated EPA regulations?
options: No — 85% exceeds the 80% minimum requirement for Type I appliances
Yes — post-1993 small appliances with functional compressors require 90% recovery
No — the 90% requirement only applies to refrigerators, not room air conditioners
Yes — all refrigerant must be recovered (100%) before any Type I system is opened
answer_text: Yes — post-1993 small appliances with functional compressors require 90% recovery
explanation: The 90% recovery requirement under 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(i) applies to ALL small appliances manufactured after November 15, 1993 with a functional compressor — including room air conditioners. Recovering only 85% falls below the 90% threshold and constitutes a violation. The technician must reach at least 90% before the system can be opened.

question: What is the EPA-required minimum refrigerant recovery percentage for a small appliance manufactured after November 15, 1993 with an operable compressor?
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(i), technicians must recover at least 90% of the refrigerant in a small appliance manufactured after November 15, 1993 when the appliance compressor is operable (functional). This is the most common Type I recovery scenario for modern equipment. The 80% standard applies when the compressor is inoperable or the equipment was manufactured before November 15, 1993.

question: In 2026, a technician services a 2018 household refrigerator using R-134a with a working compressor. The refrigerator contains 4.5 oz of refrigerant. What is the minimum amount that must be recovered?
options: 3.6 oz (80%) — because R-134a is an HFC and the rules changed in 2026
4.05 oz (90%) — post-1993 appliance with functional compressor requires 90%
4.5 oz (100%) — all refrigerant must be recovered from HFC systems
No recovery required — R-134a is not an ozone-depleting substance
answer_text: 4.05 oz (90%) — post-1993 appliance with functional compressor requires 90%
explanation: The 90% recovery requirement per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(i) applies to all small appliances manufactured after November 15, 1993 with a functional compressor — regardless of refrigerant type (CFC, HCFC, or HFC). A 2018 refrigerator with a working compressor requires 90% recovery: 4.5 oz × 0.90 = 4.05 oz minimum. R-134a is an HFC covered by Section 608 requirements, and these requirements were not changed by 2026 regulations.

question: A technician in 2026 is disposing of a 2020 window air conditioner with a working compressor. What recovery requirement applies, and does the A2L transition affect this requirement for R-32 units?
options: 90% recovery required; A2L classification of R-32 does not change the 90% threshold
80% recovery required; A2L refrigerants have a lower recovery requirement due to flammability concerns
100% recovery required; R-32's A2L status triggers a zero-emission standard
No recovery required; window AC units manufactured after 2020 are exempt under AIM Act
answer_text: 90% recovery required; A2L classification of R-32 does not change the 90% threshold
explanation: For a post-1993 small appliance with a functional compressor, 90% recovery is required per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(i) regardless of refrigerant type. While R-32 (A2L) requires spark-free service equipment per ASHRAE 15-2022, the refrigerant recovery percentage threshold remains 90% — the A2L classification does not create a different recovery standard. AIM Act changes target production/GWP limits, not recovery percentages.

question: A 1996 household refrigerator with a burned-out compressor contains 6 oz of R-134a. What is the minimum refrigerant that must be recovered before disposal?
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(ii), when a small appliance manufactured after November 15, 1993 has a non-functional (inoperable) compressor, the technician must recover at least 80% of the refrigerant. For 6 oz: 6 oz × 0.80 = 4.8 oz minimum. The non-functional compressor reduces the standard from 90% to 80% because passive recovery methods must be used. The 1996 manufacture date confirms it is post-1993.

question: A 2010 small appliance has a locked compressor (non-functional) and contains 4 oz of refrigerant. The technician recovers 3.5 oz. Has the recovery requirement been met?
options: Yes — 3.5 oz exceeds 80% (3.2 oz) of the 4-oz charge
No — a non-functional compressor triggers a 90% requirement (3.6 oz needed)
Yes — any recovery exceeding 75% is compliant for appliances with failed compressors
No — 100% must be recovered when the compressor is non-functional
answer_text: Yes — 3.5 oz exceeds 80% (3.2 oz) of the 4-oz charge
explanation: For a post-1993 small appliance with a non-functional compressor, 80% recovery is required per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(ii). The minimum recovery is 4 oz × 0.80 = 3.2 oz. The technician recovered 3.5 oz (87.5%), which exceeds the 80% minimum. A non-functional compressor REDUCES the requirement from 90% to 80% because passive recovery methods are less efficient.

question: What minimum recovery percentage applies to a post-November 15, 1993 small appliance whose compressor has failed?
options: 90% — same as for functional compressors
80% — the standard is reduced for inoperable compressors
70% — inoperable compressors allow a further reduction
100% — no refrigerant may be released when the compressor cannot assist recovery
answer_text: 80% — the standard is reduced for inoperable compressors
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(ii), when a small appliance manufactured after November 15, 1993 has an inoperable compressor, the recovery requirement is reduced to 80% (from the 90% required with a functional compressor). This reduction acknowledges that passive recovery from an appliance without a working compressor is more difficult and less efficient.

question: Why does EPA allow a lower recovery percentage (80% vs 90%) for small appliances with inoperable compressors?
options: Because refrigerant in failed compressor systems is less environmentally harmful
Because passive recovery methods used when the compressor cannot assist are less efficient
Because inoperable compressor systems always have less refrigerant remaining
Because the 90% standard was created only for systems with rotary compressors
answer_text: Because passive recovery methods used when the compressor cannot assist are less efficient
explanation: When a small appliance compressor is inoperable, the technician cannot use the system's own compressor to assist in recovery. Passive recovery methods (such as chilling the recovery cylinder to create a pressure differential) are inherently less efficient than active compression-assisted recovery. EPA acknowledges this practical limitation by allowing 80% recovery per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(ii), compared to 90% for functional-compressor systems.

question: A technician is scrapping a 2015 dehumidifier with a seized compressor. Which recovery method can the technician use since the compressor cannot operate?
options: The technician cannot recover refrigerant without a working compressor — the unit must be sent to a licensed disposal facility
The technician may use passive recovery by chilling the recovery cylinder to create a pressure differential that draws refrigerant out
The technician must replace the compressor first before any recovery can take place
The technician may vent the remaining refrigerant since the compressor is non-functional
answer_text: The technician may use passive recovery by chilling the recovery cylinder to create a pressure differential that draws refrigerant out
explanation: For Type I appliances with inoperable compressors, technicians can use passive recovery: placing the recovery cylinder in ice water or a cool location creates a pressure differential that causes refrigerant to migrate from the warmer system to the cooler cylinder. This passive method is recognized by EPA and allows the technician to meet the 80% recovery standard without an operable system compressor. Venting is never permitted per 40 CFR §82.156.

question: A technician arrives to service a 2019 room air conditioner and discovers the compressor has burned out. The unit contains 10 oz of refrigerant. What is the technician's minimum recovery obligation?
options: 9 oz (90%) — post-1993 systems always require 90% regardless of compressor condition
8 oz (80%) — post-1993 system with non-functional compressor requires 80%
10 oz (100%) — all refrigerant must be recovered before disposal of any appliance
No recovery required — a burned-out compressor qualifies the system for EPA exemption
answer_text: 8 oz (80%) — post-1993 system with non-functional compressor requires 80%
explanation: The 2019 room AC is post-November 15, 1993. Since the compressor has burned out (non-functional), the recovery requirement drops from 90% to 80% per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(ii). The technician must recover at least 10 oz × 0.80 = 8 oz. There is no EPA exemption for failed compressors — recovery is always required. A burned-out compressor means the technician must use passive recovery methods.

question: What is the minimum refrigerant recovery percentage for a small appliance manufactured before November 15, 1993?
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(iii), small appliances manufactured before November 15, 1993 must have at least 80% of their refrigerant recovered regardless of whether the compressor is functional or not. Unlike post-1993 equipment (where compressor status affects the standard: 90% functional / 80% non-functional), pre-1993 equipment applies a flat 80% requirement in all conditions.

question: A technician services a small appliance manufactured in 1987 with a working compressor. What recovery standard applies?
options: 90% — pre-1993 equipment with a functional compressor requires the same 90% as post-1993
80% — pre-1993 small appliances require 80% regardless of compressor status
No requirement — pre-1993 appliances predate the venting prohibition and are exempt
100% — older equipment with CFC refrigerants requires complete recovery
answer_text: 80% — pre-1993 small appliances require 80% regardless of compressor status
explanation: For small appliances manufactured before November 15, 1993, EPA requires 80% recovery regardless of whether the compressor is functional or not per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(iii). The compressor-status distinction (90% vs 80%) only applies to post-1993 equipment. Pre-1993 equipment always uses the 80% standard. The venting prohibition applies to ALL equipment regardless of manufacture date.

question: A technician needs to service three small appliances: a 1990 refrigerator (working compressor), a 1991 freezer (dead compressor), and a 1992 window AC (working compressor). What single recovery percentage applies to all three?
options: 90% for all — all appliances require 90% minimum recovery
80% for the one with the dead compressor, 90% for the other two
80% for all — all three are pre-1993, so 80% applies regardless of compressor status
100% for all pre-1993 appliances using CFC refrigerants
answer_text: 80% for all — all three are pre-1993, so 80% applies regardless of compressor status
explanation: All three appliances were manufactured before November 15, 1993. Per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(iii), pre-1993 small appliances require 80% refrigerant recovery regardless of compressor status. The 90% requirement and the compressor-status distinction only apply to appliances manufactured after November 15, 1993. All three pre-1993 units use a flat 80% standard.

question: A 1989 refrigerator contains 12 oz of R-12. The compressor is functional. What is the minimum amount of R-12 that must be recovered?
options: 10.8 oz (90%) — functional compressor requires 90%
9.6 oz (80%) — pre-1993 appliances require 80% regardless of compressor status
12 oz (100%) — CFC refrigerants must be fully recovered
7.2 oz (60%) — older appliances with CFCs have reduced requirements
answer_text: 9.6 oz (80%) — pre-1993 appliances require 80% regardless of compressor status
explanation: The 1989 refrigerator was manufactured before November 15, 1993. Per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(iii), pre-1993 small appliances have an 80% recovery standard regardless of compressor condition. For 12 oz: 12 oz × 0.80 = 9.6 oz minimum. Even though the compressor is functional, the 90% standard does NOT apply to pre-1993 equipment — the manufacture date determines which standard applies.

question: What is the key regulatory date that determines whether a small appliance must meet the 80% or 90% recovery standard based on compressor functionality?
options: January 1, 1994
November 15, 1993
July 1, 1992
January 1, 1996
answer_text: November 15, 1993
explanation: November 15, 1993 is the critical cut-off date under 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2). Appliances manufactured after November 15, 1993 must meet either 90% (functional compressor) or 80% (non-functional compressor) recovery. Appliances manufactured before this date use a flat 80% regardless of compressor status. This date aligns with when the EPA's Section 608 recovery and recycling regulations took effect.

question: What is the alternative recovery standard for small appliances using a system-dependent recovery device?
options: Recover refrigerant until system pressure reaches 0 psig
Recover refrigerant until system pressure drops to 4 inches of mercury vacuum
Recover until only 10% of refrigerant remains in the system
Recover until the recovery cylinder is 80% full by weight
answer_text: Recover refrigerant until system pressure drops to 4 inches of mercury vacuum
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2), technicians using a system-dependent (passive) recovery device on small appliances have an alternative standard: they must recover refrigerant until the system pressure reaches 4 inches of mercury vacuum (4 in Hg). This alternative applies when the appliance compressor is used to assist recovery into an external recovery device. The 4 in Hg vacuum standard demonstrates that sufficient refrigerant has been removed.

question: A technician uses a system-dependent recovery device to recover refrigerant from a post-1993 small appliance with a functional compressor. When is the recovery complete?
options: When 90% of the refrigerant by weight has been recovered
When system pressure is reduced to 4 inches of mercury vacuum
When the manifold gauge reads 0 psig on both high and low side
When the recovery cylinder weight increase equals the manufacturer's stated charge
answer_text: When system pressure is reduced to 4 inches of mercury vacuum
explanation: When using a system-dependent recovery device (one that uses the appliance's own compressor to push refrigerant into the recovery cylinder), the alternative recovery standard under 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2) is met when system pressure reaches 4 inches of mercury vacuum. This pressure-based standard is an alternative to the weight-based 90% standard. Either method is acceptable; the technician chooses which to use.

question: A technician has reached a system pressure of 4 inches of mercury vacuum when recovering refrigerant from a small appliance with a system-dependent device. Has the recovery requirement been met?
options: Not necessarily — the technician must also verify that 90% of refrigerant by weight was captured
Yes — 4 in Hg vacuum using a system-dependent device meets the alternative recovery standard
No — 4 in Hg is not deep enough; the system must reach 10 in Hg vacuum
Yes, but only if the appliance was manufactured before November 15, 1993
answer_text: Yes — 4 in Hg vacuum using a system-dependent device meets the alternative recovery standard
explanation: When using a system-dependent recovery device on small appliances, achieving 4 inches of mercury vacuum meets the recovery standard under 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2). This is an alternative to the percentage-based standard (90% functional / 80% non-functional). The technician does NOT need to independently verify the weight recovered — reaching 4 in Hg vacuum is sufficient. The 10 in Hg standard applies to high-pressure Type II systems, not Type I.

question: A technician recovers refrigerant from a post-1993 small appliance using its compressor as part of a system-dependent recovery device. The scale shows 85% by weight recovered, but system pressure is only at 2 in Hg vacuum (not yet 4 in Hg). Is recovery complete?
options: Yes — 85% exceeds the 80% minimum and is close to 90%
No — when using a system-dependent device, recovery must continue until 4 in Hg vacuum is achieved
Yes — for post-1993 appliances with functional compressors, the 90% weight standard is optional
No — the technician must achieve both 90% by weight AND 4 in Hg vacuum
answer_text: No — when using a system-dependent device, recovery must continue until 4 in Hg vacuum is achieved
explanation: When using a system-dependent recovery device, the applicable standard is 4 inches of mercury vacuum per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2) — NOT the percentage-based standard. Even though 85% by weight has been recovered, if the system hasn't reached 4 in Hg, the alternative standard has not been met. The technician must continue until the pressure reaches 4 in Hg. The two standards (weight % and 4-in-Hg) are alternatives: you use one method, not a combination.

question: What instrument does a technician use to verify that the 4 in Hg vacuum alternative recovery standard has been met for a Type I small appliance?
options: A refrigerant scale to weigh the recovered refrigerant
A compound gauge or vacuum gauge that reads in inches of mercury
An electronic thermometer measuring system temperature
A clamp-style ammeter measuring compressor motor current
answer_text: A compound gauge or vacuum gauge that reads in inches of mercury
explanation: To verify that system pressure has reached 4 inches of mercury vacuum (the alternative recovery standard for system-dependent devices), the technician uses a compound gauge or vacuum gauge calibrated in inches of mercury. Compound gauges read both positive pressure (psig) and vacuum (in Hg), making them suitable for verifying the 4 in Hg vacuum endpoint. A refrigerant scale measures weight but is not used for the 4-in-Hg alternative standard verification.

question: A technician needs to identify the refrigerant in an older small appliance without a nameplate. What tool and method can be used?
options: A refrigerant identifier device that tests a sample of the refrigerant
A manifold gauge set — comparing measured system pressure to known saturation temperatures for common refrigerants
A UV light to detect fluorescent dye injected by the previous technician
A clamp-style ammeter measuring compressor winding resistance
answer_text: A manifold gauge set — comparing measured system pressure to known saturation temperatures for common refrigerants
explanation: In the field, technicians identify refrigerant type in small appliances by using a manifold gauge to measure system pressure at known ambient temperature, then comparing to pressure-temperature (P-T) charts for common refrigerants (CFC-12, R-134a, R-22). Each refrigerant has a unique saturation pressure at a given temperature, allowing identification. A dedicated refrigerant identifier is more definitive but may not always be available; the P-T comparison method is the standard field technique.

question: What is the approximate static (system-off) saturation pressure of R-12 (CFC-12) at 70°F (21°C) ambient temperature?
options: Approximately 70 psig
Approximately 210 psig
Approximately 10 psig
Approximately 500 psig
answer_text: Approximately 70 psig
explanation: At 70°F (21°C), R-12 (CFC-12) has a saturation pressure of approximately 70 psig. For comparison at the same temperature: R-134a is approximately 60 psig and R-22 approximately 68 psig (close to R-12, requiring a refrigerant identifier for definitive identification); R-410A is approximately 210 psig; CO2 (R-744) is approximately 840 psig. Technicians use P-T charts to estimate refrigerant type from measured pressures at known temperatures, but electronic refrigerant identifiers provide the most reliable confirmation.

question: Why is it important for a Type I technician to correctly identify the refrigerant before recovery?
options: Different refrigerants require different recovery equipment certifications
Different refrigerants must be stored in separate, labeled cylinders to prevent contamination
Only CFC refrigerants require recovery — HFC refrigerants may be vented from small appliances
The recovery percentage (80% or 90%) depends on which refrigerant is in the system
answer_text: Different refrigerants must be stored in separate, labeled cylinders to prevent contamination
explanation: Mixing different refrigerants in a recovery cylinder contaminates the refrigerant, making reclamation and reuse difficult or impossible. Before recovery, identifying the refrigerant ensures it is stored in the correct, properly labeled cylinder (required to be labeled with the refrigerant type). Refrigerant identification also helps with proper disposal and potential reuse. The recovery percentage (80%/90%) is determined by appliance age and compressor status, not refrigerant type.

question: A technician checks a small appliance and finds the low-side gauge reads below 0 psig (negative pressure / vacuum). What does this most likely indicate?
options: The system contains R-410A, which normally operates at very high pressures
The system has lost its refrigerant charge and is operating below atmospheric pressure
The system is operating normally — all small appliances operate in deep vacuum
The refrigerant has migrated to the high-pressure side, indicating a stuck expansion valve
answer_text: The system has lost its refrigerant charge and is operating below atmospheric pressure
explanation: When a small appliance low-side gauge reads below 0 psig (in vacuum/negative pressure range), it typically indicates that the system has lost most or all of its refrigerant charge. Common refrigerants in small appliances (R-12, R-134a) operate at positive pressures at ambient temperatures. A vacuum reading means air or moisture may have entered the system, and the refrigerant charge is depleted — a condition requiring further diagnosis and potential system repair.

question: What information does a pressure-temperature (P-T) chart provide that is useful for Type I small appliance technicians?
options: The maximum fill weight for refrigerant recovery cylinders
The saturation pressure of a refrigerant at a specific temperature, used to identify refrigerant type
The required evacuation depth before recharging any small appliance
The approved disposal temperature for CFC refrigerants
answer_text: The saturation pressure of a refrigerant at a specific temperature, used to identify refrigerant type
explanation: A pressure-temperature (P-T) chart shows the saturation (boiling/condensing) pressure of a refrigerant at specific temperatures. By measuring system pressure at a known temperature and comparing to a P-T chart, technicians can estimate which refrigerant is in an unidentified system. Each refrigerant has a unique P-T relationship — this relationship is the basis for pressure-based identification of refrigerants in the field.
source_ref: EPA 608 Technician Study Guide; ASHRAE Standard 34

question: What is the primary method of passive recovery used for small appliances with inoperative compressors?
options: Injecting nitrogen to push refrigerant into the recovery cylinder
Chilling the recovery cylinder with ice or cold water to create a lower-pressure destination for refrigerant
Heating the system with a heat gun to boil the refrigerant out quickly
Using a separate vacuum pump to draw refrigerant from the system
answer_text: Chilling the recovery cylinder with ice or cold water to create a lower-pressure destination for refrigerant
explanation: Passive recovery relies on pressure differential: refrigerant migrates from higher pressure (warm appliance) to lower pressure (chilled recovery cylinder). By placing the recovery cylinder in ice or cold water, the technician lowers its internal pressure, creating a gradient that draws refrigerant from the appliance. No compressor or power is needed — this method is specifically designed for appliances with inoperable compressors. Heating the system would speed migration but risks overpressure in the recovery cylinder.

question: A technician uses passive recovery (chilling the cylinder) to recover refrigerant from a small appliance with a dead compressor and achieves 79% recovery. Is this compliant?
options: Yes — passive recovery only needs to reach 75% for inoperative compressors
No — 80% is required even for inoperative compressor systems (post-1993)
Yes — 79% is within an acceptable margin of error for passive recovery
No — inoperative compressor systems require 90% just like functional compressor systems
answer_text: No — 80% is required even for inoperative compressor systems (post-1993)
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.156(a)(2)(ii), post-1993 small appliances with non-functional compressors require 80% recovery minimum — there is no margin of error or exception for passive recovery difficulty. At 79%, the technician has not met the 80% requirement. The technician must continue attempting recovery (additional passive cycles with a colder cylinder) or use alternative methods to reach the 80% threshold.

question: During passive recovery from a small appliance with an inoperative compressor, how can a technician speed up the refrigerant migration rate?
options: Apply a heat gun directly to the refrigerant lines to boil the refrigerant
Warm the appliance slightly (e.g., with warm water) and keep the recovery cylinder colder to increase the pressure differential
Open the access valve fully and wait — the refrigerant will eventually equalize without any intervention
Add more refrigerant to increase system pressure and force faster recovery
answer_text: Warm the appliance slightly (e.g., with warm water) and keep the recovery cylinder colder to increase the pressure differential
explanation: Passive recovery rate depends on the pressure differential between the appliance (higher pressure/temperature) and recovery cylinder (lower pressure/temperature). Gently warming the appliance (e.g., warm water bath) raises system pressure, while keeping the recovery cylinder colder (ice water) lowers cylinder pressure. The larger the pressure differential, the faster the refrigerant migrates. Extreme heat (heat gun) risks overpressure; adding refrigerant would violate regulations.

question: A technician is disposing of a large batch of old refrigerators with non-functional compressors. They decide to skip recovery and send the units directly to a recycler. Is this acceptable?
options: Yes — appliances going to a certified recycler are exempt from EPA recovery requirements
No — recovery is required before disposal regardless of the disposal method or destination
Yes — non-functional compressors make full recovery impossible, creating a de minimis exemption
Yes — R-12 refrigerators manufactured before 1994 are exempt from recovery requirements
answer_text: No — recovery is required before disposal regardless of the disposal method or destination
explanation: EPA regulations require refrigerant recovery before appliance disposal regardless of whether the unit is going to a recycler, landfill, or scrap yard. While some states have certified appliance dismantler programs (under 40 CFR §82.156(f)), the technician or disposal entity must still ensure refrigerant is recovered before the refrigerant-containing components are destroyed. There is no blanket exemption simply because the unit is going to a recycler. Skipping recovery violates 40 CFR §82.156.

question: Which type of recovery device is specifically designed for small appliance (Type I) recovery and can work when the system compressor is inoperable?
options: A self-contained recovery machine with its own compressor
A system-dependent recovery device that relies on the appliance compressor — only works if compressor is functional
A passive recovery device using temperature differential — works even without a functioning compressor
A nitrogen-assisted purge recovery device approved only for large commercial systems
answer_text: A passive recovery device using temperature differential — works even without a functioning compressor
explanation: Passive recovery devices for Type I appliances use temperature/pressure differential rather than an active compressor. By chilling the recovery cylinder, refrigerant migrates from the warmer appliance to the cooler cylinder without any mechanical assistance. This method works regardless of appliance compressor status. System-dependent devices use the appliance compressor, so they only work when the compressor is functional.

subtopic: Access valve installation on sealed systems
question: Small appliances like household refrigerators are hermetically sealed with no service ports. What type of access valve is typically installed to recover refrigerant?
options: A ball-type isolation valve brazed onto the suction line
A piercing-type access valve (saddle valve) that penetrates the refrigerant line without brazing
A Schrader valve welded to the compressor discharge port
A manually operated shut-off valve installed at the factory
answer_text: A piercing-type access valve (saddle valve) that penetrates the refrigerant line without brazing
explanation: Hermetically sealed small appliances have no factory-installed service ports. Technicians install piercing-type access valves (also called saddle valves or piercing valves) that clamp onto the refrigerant line and pierce it with a needle to create access — no brazing or cutting is required. These valves allow attachment of recovery equipment without permanently opening the system. After recovery, the valve is either removed with a plug or left in place.

subtopic: Access valve installation on sealed systems
question: After installing a piercing-type access valve on a household refrigerator to recover refrigerant, the technician decides to leave the valve in place and not repair the sealed system. What must the technician do?
options: Nothing — leaving the valve in place is acceptable standard practice
Remove the valve and seal the line with a proper fitting or cap to prevent future refrigerant leakage
Add a dye capsule to detect future leaks through the pierced valve
File a service record with EPA within 30 days noting the valve installation
answer_text: Remove the valve and seal the line with a proper fitting or cap to prevent future refrigerant leakage
explanation: Piercing-type access valves (saddle valves) are not designed for permanent installation — they can leak over time. If the system is being disposed of or if the technician will not repair it, the pierced line must be properly sealed to prevent refrigerant from leaking. Leaving an improperly sealed penetration in a refrigerant system creates an ongoing leak path, which violates the venting prohibition and good practice standards.

subtopic: Access valve installation on sealed systems
question: When installing a piercing access valve on a small appliance's process tube for recovery, where should the valve be placed?
options: On the high-pressure discharge line for faster recovery
On the low-pressure suction or process tube closest to the compressor inlet
On the liquid line between the condenser and expansion device
On the condenser outlet for maximum refrigerant capture
answer_text: On the low-pressure suction or process tube closest to the compressor inlet
explanation: For small appliance recovery, the access valve is typically installed on the low-pressure process tube (suction side) closest to the compressor. This location allows the compressor to push refrigerant vapor toward the recovery point. The suction side typically carries refrigerant as a low-pressure vapor, which is safer to pierce (lower pressure = less risk of sudden release). Most manufacturers place a process tube (Schrader-less stub tube) on the suction side for exactly this purpose.

subtopic: Access valve installation on sealed systems
question: Why do some small appliance process tubes require a piercing-type access valve rather than a standard Schrader valve connection?
options: Schrader valves are only rated for high-pressure systems; piercing valves are required below 15 psig
Small appliances are hermetically sealed at the factory without service ports, requiring a piercing method to gain refrigerant access
Piercing valves are cheaper and faster to install than Schrader valves
EPA regulations prohibit Schrader valves on systems with less than 5 lbs of refrigerant
answer_text: Small appliances are hermetically sealed at the factory without service ports, requiring a piercing method to gain refrigerant access
explanation: Small appliances are factory-manufactured as hermetically sealed systems — they have no built-in service ports (Schrader valves) because they are not designed to be serviced in the field. To recover refrigerant, technicians must create access to the refrigerant circuit. Piercing-type access valves (saddle valves) clamp onto the existing tubing and pierce it to create a connection point. This is the only way to access the refrigerant without cutting or brazing the sealed system.

question: Which refrigerant replaced CFC-12 (R-12) as the primary refrigerant in new household refrigerators and window air conditioners?
explanation: R-134a (1,1,1,2-tetrafluoroethane, HFC-134a) replaced CFC-12 (R-12) as the primary refrigerant in household refrigerators and automotive air conditioning systems after the CFC phaseout beginning in the early 1990s. R-134a has zero ozone depletion potential (ODP = 0) compared to R-12 (ODP = 1.0), though it has a GWP of 1430. R-134a and R-12 have similar thermodynamic properties, making the transition relatively straightforward for small appliance manufacturers.
source_ref: 40 CFR Part 82 Appendix A; EPA SNAP Program

question: Compared to CFC-12 (R-12), what is the key environmental advantage of R-134a as a refrigerant for small appliances?
options: R-134a has a lower GWP than R-12 and is safer for the climate
R-134a has zero ozone depletion potential (ODP) while R-12 has an ODP of 1.0
R-134a is a natural refrigerant with no environmental impact
R-134a is fully biodegradable and breaks down within months of release
answer_text: R-134a has zero ozone depletion potential (ODP) while R-12 has an ODP of 1.0
explanation: The primary environmental advantage of R-134a over CFC-12 (R-12) is its zero ozone depletion potential. R-12 contains chlorine atoms that destroy stratospheric ozone (ODP = 1.0, the reference compound). R-134a is a pure HFC with no chlorine, giving it zero ODP. However, R-134a still has a high global warming potential (GWP ≈ 1430), which is why next-generation alternatives like HFOs are being developed for small appliance applications.
source_ref: 40 CFR Part 82 Appendix A; Montreal Protocol

question: Can R-134a be used as a direct drop-in replacement for R-12 in an existing R-12 small appliance without any system modifications?
options: Yes — R-134a and R-12 have identical properties and are fully interchangeable
No — R-134a requires different lubricating oil and operates at different pressures than R-12
Yes — any HFC refrigerant can replace any CFC refrigerant without modification
No — R-134a is flammable and cannot be used in residential refrigerators
answer_text: No — R-134a requires different lubricating oil and operates at different pressures than R-12
explanation: R-134a is NOT a direct drop-in replacement for R-12 in existing equipment. Key differences include: (1) oil compatibility — R-12 systems use mineral oil while R-134a requires POE (polyolester) oil; (2) different saturation pressures at equivalent temperatures; (3) fitting incompatibility in some cases. A system designed for R-12 requires flushing of old mineral oil and replacement with POE oil, new filter-drier, and possibly other modifications before R-134a can be used. Retrofitting is possible but not plug-and-play.
source_ref: EPA SNAP Program; EPA 608 Technician Study Guide

question: Which lubricating oil type is compatible with R-134a in small appliance refrigeration systems?
options: Mineral oil — compatible with all HFC refrigerants
Alkylbenzene oil — the universal oil used for both CFC and HFC systems
Polyolester (POE) oil — required for compatibility with HFC-134a
Polyvinyl ether (PVE) oil — required only for auto AC systems using R-134a
answer_text: Polyolester (POE) oil — required for compatibility with HFC-134a
explanation: R-134a (HFC) requires polyolester (POE) oil because POE is miscible with HFC refrigerants, ensuring proper lubrication throughout the refrigerant circuit. Mineral oil — used with CFC-12 (R-12) — is not miscible with R-134a and will separate out and accumulate in heat exchangers, causing poor performance and compressor damage. When retrofitting R-12 systems to R-134a, all mineral oil must be flushed and replaced with POE oil.
source_ref: EPA 608 Technician Study Guide; ASHRAE Refrigerant Handbook

question: What is the ozone depletion potential (ODP) of R-134a?
options: 0 (zero ODP)
0.055 (same as R-22)
1.0 (same as R-11 and R-12)
0.5 (moderate ozone impact)
answer_text: 0 (zero ODP)
explanation: R-134a (HFC-134a) has an ozone depletion potential (ODP) of zero. As a pure HFC (hydrofluorocarbon), R-134a contains no chlorine or bromine atoms — the elements responsible for ozone destruction. This zero ODP was the primary reason R-134a was selected to replace R-12 (ODP = 1.0) in household refrigerators and automotive AC systems under the Montreal Protocol phaseout schedule.
source_ref: 40 CFR Part 82 Appendix A; Montreal Protocol

subtopic: Decomposition products when refrigerant contacts flame
question: What toxic gas is produced when CFC or HCFC refrigerants come into contact with an open flame or very hot surface?
options: Carbon dioxide (CO2)
Phosgene (COCl2) and other toxic chlorinated compounds
Methane (CH4)
Ozone (O3)
answer_text: Phosgene (COCl2) and other toxic chlorinated compounds
explanation: When chlorinated refrigerants (CFCs such as R-12, or HCFCs such as R-22) contact an open flame, torch, or hot surface, they decompose to form toxic compounds — most notably phosgene (COCl₂), a highly toxic gas. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and other chlorinated byproducts are also formed. Phosgene was used as a chemical weapon in World War I. Technicians must never use an open flame near refrigerant or in a refrigerant-contaminated atmosphere. A burning or acrid odor near an appliance indicates this decomposition is occurring.
source_ref: 40 CFR §82.156; EPA 608 Technician Study Guide; Refrigerant SDS

subtopic: Decomposition products when refrigerant contacts flame
question: A technician is using a torch near a refrigerator compressor and smells a sharp, acrid odor. What does this indicate?
options: The refrigerant charge is running low and needs to be topped off
Refrigerant is decomposing in the flame and producing toxic gases — the technician should immediately evacuate the area
The compressor oil is burning, which is normal during torch work near compressors
The solder flux is outgassing, which is harmless and expected
answer_text: Refrigerant is decomposing in the flame and producing toxic gases — the technician should immediately evacuate the area
explanation: A sharp, acrid smell when using an open flame near refrigerant lines or components indicates refrigerant decomposition. CFC and HCFC refrigerants produce phosgene (COCl₂) and other toxic gases when exposed to flame. The technician must immediately stop torch work, evacuate the area, and ventilate. Even low concentrations of phosgene can be dangerous after a time delay — symptoms may not appear immediately. Recovery of refrigerant before using any open flame is the correct procedure.
source_ref: EPA 608 Technician Study Guide; Refrigerant SDS

subtopic: Decomposition products when refrigerant contacts flame
question: Before using a torch or open flame on a small appliance refrigerant line, what must the technician do first?
options: Wear a full-face respirator rated for phosgene exposure
Recover the refrigerant from the system to prevent toxic decomposition during torch work
Apply non-combustible insulation around the brazing area to contain any gas release
Open all doors and windows to provide adequate ventilation before beginning torch work
answer_text: Recover the refrigerant from the system to prevent toxic decomposition during torch work
explanation: Before any torch or open-flame work on a refrigerant-containing system, all refrigerant must be recovered. If refrigerant remains in the system and contacts the torch flame, it will decompose into toxic gases including phosgene. Recovery eliminates the refrigerant that could come in contact with the flame. This is both an EPA regulatory requirement (no venting) and a critical safety requirement. Ventilation helps but is not a substitute for prior recovery.

subtopic: Decomposition products when refrigerant contacts flame
question: Why is phosgene particularly dangerous even when a technician notices only a faint odor?
options: Phosgene is odorless — a faint smell actually indicates a different, less dangerous gas
Phosgene has a delayed toxic effect: lung damage may not appear until hours after exposure, making it easy to underestimate exposure
Phosgene causes immediate unconsciousness, making the worker unable to escape the area
Phosgene is only dangerous at very high concentrations (above 1000 ppm); low concentrations are harmless
answer_text: Phosgene has a delayed toxic effect: lung damage may not appear until hours after exposure, making it easy to underestimate exposure
explanation: Phosgene (COCl₂) is particularly hazardous because of its delayed toxicity. After exposure, the victim may feel fine initially, but pulmonary edema (fluid in the lungs) can develop hours later and become life-threatening. This delayed response makes it dangerous: technicians may not realize they received a significant dose until symptoms appear much later. At low concentrations, phosgene has a faint hay-like or freshly cut grass odor. Immediate evacuation and medical attention are required even with apparently minor exposure.
source_ref: Refrigerant SDS; NIOSH Pocket Guide to Chemical Hazards

subtopic: Decomposition products when refrigerant contacts flame
question: What is the safest practice when a leak detector indicates refrigerant near an open flame (such as a gas heater or torch)?
options: Continue working quickly and finish before refrigerant concentration builds up
Immediately extinguish the flame and ventilate the area before proceeding
Increase airflow with a fan to dilute the refrigerant concentration near the flame
The combination is only dangerous with CFC refrigerants — HFCs are safe near flames
answer_text: Immediately extinguish the flame and ventilate the area before proceeding
explanation: Whenever refrigerant is detected near an open flame, the immediate priority is to extinguish the flame to prevent decomposition into phosgene and other toxic gases. The area must then be ventilated before continuing work. This applies to both CFC and HCFC refrigerants (which produce chlorinated decomposition products) and HFCs (which produce hydrogen fluoride, HF, when heated). No refrigerant is safe near open flames — all decompose into hazardous compounds.
source_ref: EPA 608 Technician Study Guide; Refrigerant SDS

subtopic: Decomposition products when refrigerant contacts flame
question: Which of the following correctly identifies a decomposition product of HFC refrigerants (not CFCs/HCFCs) when exposed to high heat or flame?
options: Phosgene (COCl2) — formed from fluorine atoms in HFCs at high temperature
Hydrogen fluoride (HF) — an extremely corrosive acid produced when fluorine-containing refrigerants decompose
Chlorine gas (Cl2) — released from the fluorocarbon chain in HFCs
Methane (CH4) — produced from the carbon backbone of all fluorocarbon refrigerants
answer_text: Hydrogen fluoride (HF) — an extremely corrosive acid produced when fluorine-containing refrigerants decompose
explanation: When HFC refrigerants (which contain fluorine but no chlorine) are exposed to flame or extreme heat, they decompose to produce hydrogen fluoride (HF) — an extremely corrosive and toxic acid. This is distinct from CFC/HCFC decomposition which produces phosgene (COCl₂) due to chlorine content. HF can cause severe burns and systemic toxicity. All refrigerants produce hazardous decomposition products when exposed to flame, though the specific products depend on the refrigerant's chemical composition.
source_ref: Refrigerant SDS; EPA 608 Technician Study Guide
